---
title: "Draft: Intro/Conclusions"
---

## Introduction
High Gulf water temperatures can materially affect power-plant operating capability, particularly during periods when the grid simultaneously requires peak generation. When thermal constraints tighten, the same hours that drive electricity demand upward can also coincide with reduced available capacity, creating an operational and planning risk that is both seasonal and nonlinear.

This report evaluates that risk using observed data from the Dauphin Island region. Specifically, it estimates how often Gulf water temperature exceeds the plant's 85 F (Fahrenheit) operational design assumption and how frequently those exceedance hours coincide with high electricity-demand conditions. The emphasis is on translating temperature observations into decision-relevant metrics for planning across warm-season periods and, under a conservative stationarity assumption, projecting those conditions forward over a five-year horizon.

To support this goal, the analysis follows a structured workflow. First, Gulf water temperature measurements are cleaned (including handling sentinel missingness codes), converted to Fahrenheit, and aggregated to an hourly resolution. Second, hourly electricity demand data are cleaned and time-aligned to the same local hourly grid used for the temperature series. Third, the merged dataset is used to define two operationally meaningful indicators: (1) exceedance of the 85 F threshold based on hourly water temperature, and (2) "peak-demand" hours defined as the top 10% of observed demand hours within the study period.

The report then combines descriptive and inferential analyses. It summarizes the distribution of water temperatures and demand, characterizes the seasonality of exceedances, and quantifies exceedance frequency both overall and by peak-demand status. It also fits simple logistic regression models to estimate overall exceedance probability (crude model), and to assess how exceedance likelihood changes during peak-demand hours after accounting for seasonal month effects (adjusted model). Finally, observed exceedance proportions are scaled into expected annual and five-year hours and days above the 85 F threshold to make the results directly usable for planning.

Across these methods, the results show a clear warm-season pattern in which exceedances are concentrated in late summer and early fall, with substantially lower risk in winter and spring. The analysis further indicates that exceedance risk is not uniformly distributed across demand conditions; rather, the probability and annualized frequency of exceedances are elevated during peak-demand hours--precisely the times when the grid would most benefit from full generating capacity. These findings motivate targeted operational scheduling and mitigation planning for the recurring high-risk window identified in the data.

## Conclusion
The central finding of this analysis is that Gulf water temperature exceedances of the 85 F (Fahrenheit) design assumption are highly seasonal and cluster in the warm part of the year. In the observed 2025 record, the probability that hourly temperatures exceed 85 F is meaningfully lower outside of summer/early fall, indicating that reduced-capacity events would be concentrated in specific planning periods rather than spread evenly across months.

Second, exceedance risk is materially higher during periods of elevated electricity demand. By comparing exceedance probabilities during peak-demand hours versus non-peak hours, and corroborating those patterns with simple logistic regression models, the report identifies a non-trivial subset of "most critical" hours in which both constraints are present: thermal margin is constrained (water temperature > 85 F) and the grid is simultaneously under the greatest load (top 10% demand hours). These coincident hours are operationally significant because they represent times when the plant would be least able to provide full capacity while the system most needs it.

When translated into planning terms, the observed exceedance frequency implies a recurring number of reduced-capacity operation days per year (and over the next five years under the conservative assumption that near-term conditions resemble the observed year). Importantly, a meaningful fraction of those days is expected to coincide with peak-demand conditions, strengthening the case that mitigation and outage planning should be explicitly tied to the identified high-risk window, not treated as generic scheduling constraints.

For decision-making, the results support three broad actions. First, capacity planning should incorporate the seasonally varying likelihood of exceedance-driven constraints when evaluating reserves and operational readiness. Second, operational scheduling (including maintenance and planned outages) should avoid periods when temperature exceedances and high demand are both likely. Third, risk-mitigation investments (such as cooling-system upgrades, thermal-buffering solutions, or demand-response arrangements) should be evaluated in light of both the frequency of exceedances and their coincidence with peak grid needs.

Finally, the analysis highlights clear areas for future work. The logistic models and annualization approach intentionally prioritize transparency and interpretability, but they assume that the next five years are representative of the observed conditions and that month-level adjustment adequately captures seasonality. Future work could incorporate longer historical records and climate projections, test alternative definitions of peak demand (and other operational thresholds), and apply models that directly represent plant- and system-level operational responses (e.g., considering thermal dynamics, ramping limitations, and potential lag effects between water temperature and plant performance). Together, these extensions would refine the probability estimates and improve confidence in planning decisions under changing climate and grid conditions.
